---
title: "DSCI 310: Historical Horse Population in Canada"
author: "Tiffany Timbers & Jordan Bourak"
editor: source
execute:
  echo: false
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    embed-resources: true
  pdf: default
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r}
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
```

### Aim

This project explores the historical population of horses in Canada 
between 1906 and 1972 for each province.

---

### Data

Horse population data were sourced from the 
[Government of Canada's Open Data website](http://open.canada.ca/en/open-data) 
[@horses1; @horses2].

---

### Methods

The R programming language [@R]
and the following R packages were used to perform the analysis: 
knitr [@knitr], tidyverse [@tidyverse], and Quarto [@Allaire_Quarto_2022]. 
*Note: this report is adapted from @ttimbers-horses.*

---

### Results

![Historical horse populations in Canadian provinces from 1906 to 1972.](../results/horse_pops_plot.png){#fig-provincial-horses}

As shown in @fig-provincial-horses, the number of horses recorded in each Canadian province varies across the study period. Ontario, Saskatchewan, and Alberta consistently appear among the provinces with the largest horse populations. A noticeable decline begins around the 1940s. One possible explanation for this pattern is the growing adoption of automobiles and mechanized farming equipment, which reduced the reliance on horses for transportation and agricultural work.

To further explore differences among provinces, we examine how much the horse population varied over time. The variability of the population counts is summarized using the standard deviation values shown in @tbl-prov-sd.

```{r}
#| message: false
#| label: tbl-prov-sd
#| tbl-cap: "Sample standard deviation of horse populations by Canadian province from 1906 to 1972."

horses_sd_table <- read_csv("../results/horses_sd.csv")
largest_sd <- horses_sd_table$Province[1]
knitr::kable(horses_sd_table)
```

The sample standard deviation is defined as

$$
s = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \overline{x})^2}{N-1}}
$$

These values summarize the same time period illustrated in @fig-provincial-horses.

![Horse population trend for the province with the highest variability.](../results/horse_pop_plot_largest_sd.png){#fig-largest-variation width=65%}

@fig-largest-variation focuses on the province with the greatest variation in horse population values. Based on the calculations in @tbl-prov-sd, the province with the largest standard deviation is `r largest_sd`.

### References
